title: Pasta fails with "ASSSERTION FAILED in flow_alloc (flow.c:521): flow->f.state == FLOW_STATE_FREE"
body:
### Issue Description

PiHole instance running using Podman is no longer reachable after a seemingly random amount of time.

My Fedora system updated about 8 hours ago, about 1 hour ago it experienced an issue (morning) where the PiHole instance was no longer reachable. Upon investigation there was the `ASSSERTION FAILED` entry in the service logs.

Restarting the services temporarilly fixes the issue. But after about 30min the same error occured. More traffic occured between the two failures than it did before, hence the assumption the amount of traffic might be related.



### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue (I did not re-create a smaller version, but here is how it is currently running)
1. Setup a PiHole Podman (see environment description below)
2. Use the PiHole as your DNS (more traffic seems related?)
3. Wait for Pasta to throw the Asssertion error


### Describe the results you received

The container is no longer reachable after a seemingly random amount of time, logs then show: (IDs replaced with xxxxx)
```
pasta
ASSSERTION FAILED in flow_alloc (flow.c:521): flow->f.state == FLOW_STATE_FREE
PRIORITY 2
SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER pasta
SYSLOG_RAW <2> pasta: ASSSERTION FAILED in flow_alloc (flow.c:521): flow->f.state == FLOW_STATE_FREE
_AUDIT_LOGINUID 1000
_AUDIT_SESSION 1
_BOOT_ID xxxxx
_CAP_EFFECTIVE 200400
_COMM pasta
_GID 1000
_HOSTNAME xxxxx
_MACHINE_ID xxxxx
_PID 3288
_RUNTIME_SCOPE system
_SELINUX_CONTEXT unconfined_u:unconfined_r:container_runtime_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023
_SOURCE_REALTIME_TIMESTAMP 1745475986527151
_SYSTEMD_CGROUP /user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/app.slice/pihole.service/runtime
_SYSTEMD_INVOCATION_ID xxxxx
_SYSTEMD_OWNER_UID 1000
_SYSTEMD_SLICE user-1000.slice
_SYSTEMD_UNIT user@1000.service
_SYSTEMD_USER_SLICE app.slice
_SYSTEMD_USER_UNIT pihole.service
_TRANSPORT syslog
_UID 1000
__CURSOR s=xxxxx;i=xxxxx;b=xxxxx;m=xxxxx;t=xxxxx;x=xxxxx
__MONOTONIC_TIMESTAMP 24386886488
__REALTIME_TIMESTAMP 1745475986528561
__SEQNUM 1010935
__SEQNUM_ID xxxxx
```

### Describe the results you expected

Operation without failure

### Additional environment details

HP Pavilion dm1 Notebook PC
CPU 2x AMD E2-1800 APU with Radeon(tm) HD Graphics

```

### Additional information

It now occured twice, if it never happens again or happens more often I will of course update this info here.

comments.body: I'm not sure if this is a problem on the podman side. WDYT? @Luap99
This is definitely a pasta bug and not a podman issue. But we generally accept pasta bugs here as well, just make sure to ping @sbrivio-rh and he will handle it.
Seen, thanks for the report! No questions yet.

> [@MartinAirN](https://github.com/MartinAirN) would you have a chance to:
> 
> 1. pass the `-d` (`--debug`) as well as the `--log-file` options to pasta, then pray that we hit the issue again? If you're using `podman run`, that would be for example `podman run --net=pasta:-d,--log-file,/tmp/pasta.log`. If you're starting the container in another way, the `pasta_options` array in containers.conf can be used instead
> 2. try with a slightly older version of pasta? `git bisect` would be even better (`pasta` can be built / installed / removed very easily, `git clone git://passt.top/passt && cd passt && make`, then as root: `make install` and `make uninstall` as needed, Podman will just pick it up)

I have done the first, once another failure happens I'll have a look at the logs and get them here. (I do expect one, we are on nr 5 for today)

As the system does need to be up (for now) I am a bit more hesitant on the 2nd.

As yesterday no one was really using the network, so that might be why errors on that day went undetected, but they might also not have been there.

Small update:
After enabling logging the error did not occur again (for at least 6 hours). Whilst previously it occurred at least once every 2 hours.

I'll leave it running for now.

> Small update: After enabling logging the error did not occur again (for at least 6 hours). Whilst previously it occurred at least once every 2 hours.

Thanks for the update. I guess it might somehow depend on timing.

> I'll leave it running for now.

That would be great, thanks, because I couldn't reproduce this yet, and I couldn't find a possible explanation either. I'm still trying though.

Cc: @eugpermar
@MartinAirN Could you capture the traffic either with tcpdump or pasta --pcap option, so we can check if the same flow reproduces the issue? If that is the case we could iterate way faster on this.

> I have some logs, they however do not seem very useful to me. But maybe it explains something for you. This is over a night run, so less traffic than normal.

This is great, thanks. For sure it restricts the scope quite a lot.

> [@MartinAirN](https://github.com/MartinAirN) Could you capture the traffic either with tcpdump or pasta --pcap option, so we can check if the same flow reproduces the issue? If that is the case we could iterate way faster on this.

@MartinAirN, for context, pasta can also take traffic captures, and that would be an additional `-p,/tmp/pihole.pcap` in the command line options.

> > I have some logs, they however do not seem very useful to me. But maybe it explains something for you. This is over a night run, so less traffic than normal.
> 
> This is great, thanks. For sure it restricts the scope quite a lot.
> 
> > [@MartinAirN](https://github.com/MartinAirN) Could you capture the traffic either with tcpdump or pasta --pcap option, so we can check if the same flow reproduces the issue? If that is the case we could iterate way faster on this.
> 
> [@MartinAirN](https://github.com/MartinAirN), for context, pasta can also take traffic captures, and that would be an additional `-p,/tmp/pihole.pcap` in the command line options.

I had just downgraded to `0^20240906.g6b38f07-1.fc41` to test that, guess I'll upgrade again.

Might have to keep an eye on diskspace I guess, but I'll go ahead and enable the captures. With two working from home today I guess this might happen quick, unless the timing is now even more off with more logging enabled.

The gist here is that we free two flows in a row:

> ```
> 18213.6264:          Flow 59 (UDP flow): Side 0 hash table remove: bucket: 275062
> 18213.6264:          Flow 59 (UDP flow): Side 1 hash table remove: bucket: 108243
> 18213.6265:          Flow 59 (FREE): ACTIVE -> FREE
> 18213.6265:          Flow 59 (FREE): HOST [192.168.1.25]:64152 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => TAP [192.168.1.25]:64152 -> [192.168.1.53]:53
> 18213.6265:          Flow 60 (UDP flow): Side 0 hash table remove: bucket: 150453
> 18213.6265:          Flow 60 (UDP flow): Side 1 hash table remove: bucket: 228704
> 18213.6265:          Flow 60 (FREE): ACTIVE -> FREE
> 18213.6265:          Flow 60 (FREE): HOST [192.168.1.25]:6976 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => TAP [192.168.1.25]:6976 -> [192.168.1.53]:53
> ```

Then we reuse those flow slots in the same order, first 59:

> ```
> 18213.6266:          Flow 59 (NEW): FREE -> NEW
> 18213.6266:          Flow 59 (INI): NEW -> INI
> 18213.6266:          Flow 59 (INI): HOST [192.168.1.80]:36253 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => ?
> 18213.6266:          Flow 59 (TGT): INI -> TGT
> 18213.6266:          Flow 59 (TGT): HOST [192.168.1.80]:36253 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => TAP [192.168.1.80]:36253 -> [192.168.1.53]:53
> 18213.6266:          Flow 59 (UDP flow): TGT -> TYPED
> 18213.6266:          Flow 59 (UDP flow): HOST [192.168.1.80]:36253 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => TAP [192.168.1.80]:36253 -> [192.168.1.53]:53
> 18213.6268:          Flow 59 (UDP flow): Side 0 hash table insert: bucket: 60336
> 18213.6268:          Flow 59 (UDP flow): Side 1 hash table insert: bucket: 67262
> 18213.6268:          Flow 59 (UDP flow): TYPED -> ACTIVE
> 18213.6268:          Flow 59 (UDP flow): HOST [192.168.1.80]:36253 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => TAP [192.168.1.80]:36253 -> [192.168.1.53]:53
> ```

then 60:

> ```
> 18213.6270:          Flow 60 (NEW): FREE -> NEW
> 18213.6270:          Flow 60 (INI): NEW -> INI
> 18213.6270:          Flow 60 (INI): HOST [192.168.1.80]:39886 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => ?
> 18213.6271:          Flow 60 (TGT): INI -> TGT
> 18213.6271:          Flow 60 (TGT): HOST [192.168.1.80]:39886 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => TAP [192.168.1.80]:39886 -> [192.168.1.53]:53
> 18213.6271:          Flow 60 (UDP flow): TGT -> TYPED
> 18213.6271:          Flow 60 (UDP flow): HOST [192.168.1.80]:39886 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => TAP [192.168.1.80]:39886 -> [192.168.1.53]:53
> 18213.6273:          Flow 60 (UDP flow): Side 0 hash table insert: bucket: 278654
> 18213.6273:          Flow 60 (UDP flow): Side 1 hash table insert: bucket: 297335
> 18213.6273:          Flow 60 (UDP flow): TYPED -> ACTIVE
> 18213.6273:          Flow 60 (UDP flow): HOST [192.168.1.80]:39886 -> [192.168.1.53]:53 => TAP [192.168.1.80]:39886 -> [192.168.1.53]:53
> ```

...and then another one (or maybe by mistake the same as a previous one...?):

> ```
> 18213.6274: (null)ASSSERTION FAILED in flow_alloc (flow.c:521): flow->f.state == FLOW_STATE_FREE
> ```

and for some reason in this specific case we forgot to mark one (?) slot as free, so we panic when we see that. It might very well a pre-existing issue in flow bookkeeping which might have been made more visible (or visible at all) by recent changes in UDP flow handling.

Ok, so they are still happening regularly. That is the good news. I guess you would prefer the capture unsanitized to properly reproduce.

As you might imagine I would prefer to not put that on the WWW. Do we have a way to share this directly or indeed sanitize? @sbrivio-rh (I can send a link to a fileshare of course)

> Ok, so they are still happening regularly. That is the good news.

\o/

> I guess you would prefer the capture unsanitized to properly reproduce.

It might help, I'm not sure if it's necessary to have unmodified DNS queries and IP addresses though. Maybe sanitised is also fine. No idea yet.

> As you might imagine I would prefer to not put that on the WWW. Do we have a way to share this directly or indeed sanitize? [@sbrivio-rh](https://github.com/sbrivio-rh) (I can send a link to a fileshare of course)

You can send me an email (sbrivio@redhat.com) with a link or a decryption key if you want. You can also encrypt it with GPG if you prefer, my public key is available on all the usual keyservers.

@MartinAirN Probably it can be anonymized, but I have no experience with that. If you are worried and have the option, maybe we can try to reduce the pcap so you send the less information the better.
For example, if the pcap has 1000 frames, let's try to reproduce the bug just with the last 500 ones, etc etc. Would that work for you?

>

You have mail.

The link will be valid for the next 13 hours.


In the meantime I have rolledback to `0^20240906.g6b38f07-1.fc41`.

Small update: With the `0^20240906.g6b38f07-1.fc41` version there are errors in logs, but no disruptions in service.

The errors:
`<3> pasta: Error receiving datagrams`
`<3> pasta: : Connection refused`

@MartinAirN, thanks for the update. We've been looking into this with a substantial amount of eyeballs for a while now, and yet we couldn't reproduce it, let alone find the root cause.

I prepared a debugging [patch](https://passt.top/passt/commit/?h=podman_25959&id=9c239345363f13bb63250d87af9988f150c037dc) to dump some additional information along with a core dump once the issue occurs. Would it be possible for you to try that out? It's quite simple, it should take a few seconds, and the new build can be uninstalled cleanly afterwards:

    git clone -b podman_25959 git://passt.top/passt && cd passt && make && sudo make install

then restart your Pi-hole container: Podman will pick up the new build on its own.

Once the problem occurs, some more messages will be printed in the log file, and a core dump will be generated. It can be exported using corectldump(1): list with `corectldump list`, and then export the last one, for example:

    $ coredumpctl list
    TIME                            PID  UID  GID SIG    COREFILE EXE                             SIZE
    Tue 2025-04-29 15:43:59 UTC 1240504 1001 1001 SIGSYS present  /home/sbrivio/passt/passt.avx2 32.4K
    $ coredumpctl dump 1240504 -o core.dump
    [...]

that would be immensely helpful. The backtrace of the core dump itself (also printed by corectldump) doesn't contain confidential data, the rest does, so it would help if you could share that offline.

Once you reproduced the problem, you can cleanly remove the local build from your system with `sudo make uninstall`.

> [@MartinAirN](https://github.com/MartinAirN), thanks for the update. We've been looking into this with a substantial amount of eyeballs for a while now, and yet we couldn't reproduce it, let alone find the root cause.
> 
> I prepared a debugging [patch](https://passt.top/passt/commit/?h=podman_25959&id=9c239345363f13bb63250d87af9988f150c037dc) to dump some additional information along with a core dump once the issue occurs. Would it be possible for you to try that out? It's quite simple, it should take a few seconds, and the new build can be uninstalled cleanly afterwards:
> 
> ```
> git clone -b podman_25959 git://passt.top/passt && cd passt && make && sudo make install
> ```
> 
> then restart your Pi-hole container: Podman will pick up the new build on its own.
> 
> Once the problem occurs, some more messages will be printed in the log file, and a core dump will be generated. It can be exported using corectldump(1): list with `corectldump list`, and then export the last one, for example:
> 
> ```
> $ coredumpctl list
> TIME                            PID  UID  GID SIG    COREFILE EXE                             SIZE

> 
> that would be immensely helpful. The backtrace of the core dump itself (also printed by corectldump) doesn't contain confidential data, the rest does, so it would help if you could share that offline.
> 
> Once you reproduced the problem, you can cleanly remove the local build from your system with `sudo make uninstall`.

This can be done without issue on a system with SELinux enabled? And would the basis of the most recent available version be better to be used or can I leave the currently installed (through dnf) version there to be overwritten?

> This can be done without issue on a system with SELinux enabled?

Yes, but it should be done just for testing, because if you install a binary like that, _in general_, it will start as `unconfined_t`. 

Now, given that Podman starts it, and we still have https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=81 open, it will actually run as `container_runtime_t` in any case, but I don't want to encourage bad practices.

> And would the basis of the most recent available version be better to be used or can I leave the currently installed (through dnf) version there to be overwritten?

There's no basis, and nothing is overwritten, so you can leave that one (also because it's the one working for you...). The local build is installed under `/usr/local/bin/`, while the one from the package resides under `/usr/bin/`.

It looks like @vivier was finally able to [figure out](https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20250430160525.3642997-1-lvivier@redhat.com/) the issue.

@MartinAirN, could you give that fix a try for a while, before I merge it and make a new release? We couldn't reproduce the issue yet, but we're reasonably confident that that should fix it. That would be:

    git clone git://passt.top/passt && cd passt && b4 shazam https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20250430160525.3642997-1-lvivier@redhat.com/ && make && sudo make install

(overwriting your current local build). You might need to `dnf install b4`. I can also prepare a test package if it's more convenient for you. Let me know.
